primary_reason: The paper develops TMLE parameters for effect modification in survival analysis and demonstrates them using HIV treatment data.
The study applies the parametric g-formula to time-varying confounding in HIV cohorts and compares its estimates with a marginal structural model.
The paper proposes an information criterion for marginal structural model specification and evaluates it through simulation and empirical examples.
The study combines multilocus sequence typing, spatial isolation-by-distance analysis, and host-pathogen population comparisons to investigate disease-suppressing bacterial symbionts.
The study uses longitudinal g-computation to compare dynamic CD4-based antiretroviral initiation policies while adjusting for treatment-confounder feedback.
The paper explains how the parametric g-formula can replace exposure contrasts with policy-relevant population-intervention effects in longitudinal studies.
The study demonstrates causal-diagram construction and compares g-computation, inverse weighting, and TMLE for an HIV-related cervical-disease question.
The paper compares clustered longitudinal TMLE with other causal estimators and uses simulation to examine unmeasured cluster confounding and data-adaptive estimation.
The study uses the parametric g-formula to estimate mortality under different levels of fidelity to a dynamic HIV-tuberculosis treatment policy.
The study applies the parametric g-formula to compare long-term mortality under alternative ART initiation thresholds and assess effect modification by age.
The study uses molecular docking and molecular-dynamics simulation to investigate how chemical-exposure products might inhibit immune proteins involved in tuberculosis control.
The study uses the parametric g-formula to compare immediate and CD4-triggered ART policies under baseline and time-varying confounding.
The analysis uses g-computation to estimate pediatric HIV growth and mortality under alternative treatment-initiation rules across ages and regions.
The study uses the parametric g-formula to estimate diarrheal incidence under realistic and targeted interventions preventing unnecessary early antibiotic exposure.
The multiregional analysis applies the g-formula to compare pediatric HIV mortality and growth under dynamic ART initiation criteria.
The paper develops a Bayesian nonparametric marginal structural model, evaluates its efficiency in simulations, and applies it to HIV data.
The analysis uses the parametric g-formula to estimate the per-protocol effect of immediate versus deferred ART despite protocol deviations in a randomized trial.

The study uses multilevel regression and g-computation to estimate gender-specific HIV-testing uptake under hypothetical reductions in community stigma.

The study applies the extended parametric g-formula to estimate HIV mortality under joint hypothetical interventions on ART initiation and depression.
The study uses the parametric g-formula to compare acquired HIV drug-resistance risks under alternative ART initiation strategies while adjusting for time-varying testing and clinical factors.
The paper proposes using g-formula estimates from observational data as calibration targets for individual-level disease simulation models and demonstrates the approach with HIV.
The study empirically assesses transportability of an online HIV-testing trial using inverse sampling weights and the g-formula.
The paper develops a sensitivity-analysis procedure for cause-of-death misclassification within the parametric g-formula and illustrates it for AIDS mortality.
